sourceQuestion:
An element in an array X is called a leader if it is greater than all elements to the right of it in X. The best algorithm to find all leaders in an array:

A. solves it in linear time using a left to right pass of the array
B. solves it in linear time using a right to left pass of the array
C. solves it using divide and conquer in time Theta(n log n)
D. solves it in time Theta(n^2)
sourceAnswer: B. solves it in linear time using a right to left pass of the array

parsed.stem: An element in an array X is called a leader if it is greater than all elements to the right of it in X. The best algorithm to find all leaders in an array:

options.text: solves it in linear time using a left to right pass of the array
solves it in linear time using a right to left pass of the array
solves it using divide and conquer in time Theta(n log n)

sourceQuestion:
Given two arrays of numbers a_1, ..., a_n and b_1, ..., b_n where each number is 0 or 1, the fastest algorithm to find the largest span (i, j) such that a_i + a_{i+1} + ... + a_j = b_i + b_{i+1} + ... + b_j or report that there is no such span,

A. Takes O(3^n) and Omega(2^n) time if hashing is permitted
B. Takes O(n) and Omega(n) time in the key comparison mode
C. Takes Theta(n) time and space
D. Takes O(n) time only if the sum of the 2n elements is an even number
sourceAnswer: C. Takes Theta(n) time and space

parsed.stem: Given two arrays of numbers a_1, ..., a_n and b_1, ..., b_n where each number is 0 or 1, the fastest algorithm to find the largest span (i, j) such that a_i + a_{i+1} + ... + a_j = b_i + b_{i+1} + ... + b_j or report that there is no such span,

options.text: Takes O(3^n) and Omega(2^n) time if hashing is permitted
Takes O(n) and Omega(n) time in the key comparison mode
Takes O(n) time only if the sum of the 2n elements is an even number

sourceQuestion:
Define R_n to be the maximum amount earned by cutting a rod of length n meters into one or more pieces of integer length and selling them. For i > 0, let p[i] denote the selling price of a rod whose length is i meters. Consider the array of prices:
p[1]=1, p[2]=5, p[3]=8, p[4]=9, p[5]=10, p[6]=17, p[7]=17, p[8]=20, p[9]=18

Which of the following statements is/are correct about R_7?

A. R_7 = 18
B. R_7 = 19
C. R_7 is achieved by three different optimal solutions
D. R_7 cannot be achieved by a solution consisting of three pieces

parsed.stem:
Define R_n to be the maximum amount earned by cutting a rod of length n meters into one or more pieces of integer length and selling them. For i > 0, let p[i] denote the selling price of a rod whose length is i meters. Consider the array of prices:
p[1]=1, p[2]=5, p[3]=8, p[4]=9, p[5]=10, p[6]=17, p[7]=17, p[8]=20, p[9]=18

Which of the following statements is/are correct about R_7?

options.text: R_7 is achieved by three different optimal solutions
R_7 cannot be achieved by a solution consisting of three pieces

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following problem. Given n positive integers a_1, a_2, ..., a_n, it is required to partition them into two parts A and B such that sum_{a_i in A} a_i - sum_{a_j in B} a_j is minimised.

Consider a greedy algorithm for solving this problem. Numbers are ordered so that a_1 >= a_2 >= ... >= a_n, and at the i-th step, a_i is placed in that part whose sum is smaller at that step.

Consider a greedy algorithm for solving this problem. Which of the following algorithm design techniques does the greedy algorithm use?

A. Dynamic programming
B. Backtracking
C. Divide and conquer
D. Greedy method

parsed.stem:
Consider the following problem. Given n positive integers a_1, a_2, ..., a_n, it is required to partition them into two parts A and B such that sum_{a_i in A} a_i - sum_{a_j in B} a_j is minimised.

Consider a greedy algorithm for solving this problem. Numbers are ordered so that a_1 >= a_2 >= ... >= a_n, and at the i-th step, a_i is placed in that part whose sum is smaller at that step.

Consider a greedy algorithm for solving this problem. Which of the following algorithm design techniques does the greedy algorithm use?

sourceQuestion:
Given below are some algorithms, and some algorithm design paradigms.

1. Dijkstra's Shortest Path
2. Floyd-Warshall algorithm to compute all pair shortest path
3. Binary search on a sorted array
4. Backtracking search on a graph

Design Paradigms:
i.   Divide and Conquer
ii.  Dynamic Programming
iii. Greedy design
iv.  Depth-first search
v.   Breadth-first search

Match the above algorithms on the left to the corresponding design paradigm they follow.

A. 1-i, 2-iii, 3-i, 4-v
B. 1-iii, 2-iii, 3-i, 4-v
C. 1-iii, 2-ii, 3-i, 4-iv
D. 1-iii, 2-ii, 3-i, 4-v

parsed.stem:
Given below are some algorithms, and some algorithm design paradigms.

1. Dijkstra's Shortest Path
2. Floyd-Warshall algorithm to compute all pair shortest path
3. Binary search on a sorted array
4. Backtracking search on a graph

Design Paradigms:
i.   Divide and Conquer
ii.  Dynamic Programming
iii. Greedy design
iv.  Depth-first search
v.   Breadth-first search

Match the above algorithms on the left to the corresponding design paradigm they follow.

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following table:

Algorithms          Design Paradigms
(P) Kruskal         (I)  Divide and Conquer
(Q) Quicksort       (II) Greedy
(R) Floyd-Warshall  (III) Dynamic Programming

Match the algorithms to the design paradigms they are based on.

A. (P) -> (II), (Q) -> (III), (R) -> (I)
B. (P) -> (III), (Q) -> (I), (R) -> (II)
C. (P) -> (II), (Q) -> (I), (R) -> (III)
D. (P) -> (I), (Q) -> (II), (R) -> (III)

sourceQuestion:
If T_1 = O(1), give the correct matching for the following pairs:

(M) T_n = T_{n-1} + n        (U) T_n = O(n)
(N) T_n = T_{n-1} + n        (V) T_n = O(n log n)
(O) T_n = T_{n/2} + n        (W) T_n = O(n^2)

A. M-W, N-V, O-U, P-X
B. M-W, N-U, O-X, P-V
C. M-V, N-W, O-X, P-U
D. M-W, N-U, O-X, P-V

sourceQuestion:

10, sqrt(n), n, log_2(n), 100/n

B. 100/n, 10, sqrt(n), log_2(n), n
C. 10, 100/n, sqrt(n), log_2(n), n
D. 100/n, log_2(n), 10, sqrt(n), n

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following three functions.
f_1 = 10^n
f_2 = n^(log n)
f_3 = n^(sqrt(n))

Which one of the following options arranges the functions in the increasing order of asymptotic growth rate?

A. f_1, f_2, f_3
B. f_2, f_1, f_3
C. f_2, f_3, f_1
D. f_3, f_2, f_1

Which one of the following options arranges the functions in the increasing order of asymptotic growth rate?

sourceQuestion:
Which one of the following statements is TRUE for all positive functions f(n)?

A. f(n^2) = Theta(f(n)^2) when f(n) is a polynomial
B. f(n^2) = O(f(n)^2)
C. f(n^2) = Omega(f(n)^2) when f(n) is an exponential function
D. f(n^2) = O(f(n))
sourceAnswer: A. f(n^2) = Theta(f(n)^2) when f(n) is a polynomial

parsed.stem: Which one of the following statements is TRUE for all positive functions f(n)?

options.text: f(n^2) = Theta(f(n)^2) when f(n) is a polynomial
f(n^2) = Omega(f(n)^2) when f(n) is an exponential function

sourceQuestion:
Let f and g be functions of natural numbers given by f(n) = n and g(n) = n^2. Which of the following statements is/are TRUE?

A. f = O(g)
B. f = Omega(g)
C. f = O(g^2)
D. f = Theta(g)

parsed.stem: Let f and g be functions of natural numbers given by f(n) = n and g(n) = n^2. Which of the following statements is/are TRUE?

sourceQuestion:
Consider functions Function_1 and Function_2 expressed in pseudocode as follows:

Function_1:
  while n > 1 do
    for i = 1 to 100 * n do
      x = x + 1;
    end for
    n = n / 2;
  end while

Function_2:
  for i = 1 to n do
    for j = 1 to n do
      x = x + 1;
    end for
  end for

Let f1(n) and f2(n) denote the number of times the statement 'x = x + 1' is executed in Function_1 and Function_2, respectively. Which of the following statements are TRUE?

A. f1(n) is in Theta(f2(n))
B. f1(n) is in O(f2(n))
C. f1(n) is in Theta(n^2 * log n)
D. f2(n) is in O(n)

parsed.stem:
Consider functions Function_1 and Function_2 expressed in pseudocode as follows:

Function_1:
  while n > 1 do
    for i = 1 to 100 * n do
      x = x + 1;
    end for
    n = n / 2;
  end while

Function_2:
  for i = 1 to n do
    for j = 1 to n do
      x = x + 1;
    end for
  end for

Let f1(n) and f2(n) denote the number of times the statement 'x = x + 1' is executed in Function_1 and Function_2, respectively. Which of the following statements are TRUE?

options.text: f1(n) is in Theta(n^2 * log n)

sourceQuestion:
Let T(n) be the recurrence relation defined as follows:
  T(0) = 1,
  T(1) = 2, and
  T(n) = 6*T(n-1) - 8*T(n-2)  for n >= 2

Which one of the following statements is TRUE?

A. T(n) = Theta(2^n)
B. T(n) = Theta(n*2^n)
C. T(n) = Theta(3^n)
D. T(n) = Theta(n*3^n)

Which one of the following statements is TRUE?

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following two functions:

g1(n) = n^2  for 0 <= n < 10,000
         n^3  for n >= 10,000

g2(n) = n    for 0 <= n < 100
         n^2  for n >= 100

Which of the following is true?

A. g1(n) is O(g2(n))
B. g1(n) is Omega(g2(n))
C. g2(n) is O(g1(n))
D. g2(n) is Omega(g1(n))

         n^2  for n >= 100

Which of the following is true?

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following is false?
A. 100*log(n^2) = O(log n)
B. sqrt(log n) = O(log(log n))
C. If 0 < x < y then n^x = O(n^y)
D. 2^n != O(n^k)

parsed.stem: Which of the following is false?

options.text: If 0 < x < y then n^x = O(n^y)

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following functions:
  f(n) = 3n^(1/2)
  g(n) = 2^(3*log_2(n))
  h(n) = n!

Which of the following is true?
A. h(n) is O(f(n))
B. h(n) is O(g(n))
C. g(n) is not O(f(n))
D. f(n) is O(g(n))

sourceQuestion:
Let f(n) = n^2 * log(n) and g(n) = n * (log n)^10 be two positive functions of n. Which of the following statements is correct?
A. f(n) = O(g(n)) and g(n) != O(f(n))
B. g(n) = O(f(n)) and f(n) != O(g(n))
C. f(n) = O(g(n)) and g(n) = O(f(n))
D. f(n) != O(g(n)) and g(n) != O(f(n))
sourceAnswer: B. g(n) = O(f(n)) and f(n) != O(g(n))

III. 2^(2n) = O(2^n)

Which of the following claims are correct?
A. I and II
B. I and III
C. II and III
D. I, II, and III

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following functions:
- f(n) = 2^n
- g(n) = n^2
- h(n) = n^(log n)

Which of the following statements about the asymptotic behavior of f(n), g(n) and h(n) is true?

A. f(n) = O(g(n)); g(n) = O(h(n))
B. f(n) = Theta(g(n)); g(n) = O(h(n))
C. g(n) = O(f(n)); h(n) = O(f(n))
D. h(n) = O(f(n)); g(n) = Omega(f(n))

- h(n) = n^(log n)

sourceQuestion:
Which of the given options provides the increasing order of asymptotic complexity of functions f1, f2, f3, and f4?
- f1(n) = 2^n
- f2(n) = n^(3/2)
- f3(n) = n * log(n)
- f4(n) = n^(log n)

A. f3, f2, f4, f1
B. f3, f2, f1, f4
C. f2, f3, f1, f4
D. f2, f3, f4, f1

- f2(n) = n^(3/2)
- f3(n) = n * log(n)
- f4(n) = n^(log n)

sourceQuestion:
Let W(n) and A(n) denote respectively, the worst case and average case running time of an algorithm executed on an input of size n. Which of the following is ALWAYS TRUE?

A. A(n) = Omega(W(n))
B. A(n) = Theta(W(n))
C. A(n) = O(W(n))
D. A(n) = O(sqrt(W(n)))

parsed.stem: Let W(n) and A(n) denote respectively, the worst case and average case running time of an algorithm executed on an input of size n. Which of the following is ALWAYS TRUE?
